identity.mission: The Grey Matter exists to translate rigorous psychological research into cinematic, accessible essays without losing the science underneath. The channel works from peer-reviewed journals, named studies, primary researcher interviews, and replication data — not from pop-psychology paperbacks or self-help frameworks. Every episode interrogates a question about human behaviour, cognition, or emotion by walking the viewer through what specific researchers actually found, what their methods were, what the limits of those findings are, and what we still do not know. The goal is to make the viewer smarter about the mind without overselling what the science actually proves.
identity.audience: The Grey Matter is built for curious, educated adults between 22 and 50 who read New Scientist, Aeon, Quanta, or Nautilus, who listen to Hidden Brain or Sean Carroll's Mindscape, and who are tired of self-help reductionism and clickbait psychology. The audience includes university students, lifelong learners, professionals in fields where understanding people matters (clinicians, teachers, designers, managers), and skeptics who want the science behind the slogans. They want to learn, they trust nuance, and they will leave immediately if the channel oversells a finding.
identity.mood: curious, measured, intellectually honest, cinematic, quietly humane
identity.forbidden: self-help platitudes, manifestation, law of attraction, MBTI as science, pseudoscience framings, anti-psychiatry rhetoric, evolutionary-psychology just-so stories, fitness/discipline grindset framing, alpha/sigma/masculinity content, dating advice, neuroplasticity as magic, 'left brain / right brain' myths, '10% of your brain' myths, anything that contradicts current scientific consensus without explicitly engaging with that consensus
identity.refs: Robert Sapolsky, Daniel Kahneman, Oliver Sacks, Hidden Brain, Mindscape, Sean Carroll, Aeon, Quanta Magazine, In Our Time, Radiolab, Big Think (best episodes only), Veritasium psychology episodes

script.tone:
The writing tone should be that of a thoughtful researcher giving a public lecture they have given many times before — they know the material deeply, they care about communicating it precisely, and they are not in a hurry. The voice should feel like Oliver Sacks describing a patient, or Robert Sapolsky walking through a study at Stanford. Warm, but careful. Curious, but rigorous. The viewer should leave understanding something they did not understand before, including what is still uncertain.

The script structure mirrors a long-form science essay:

The phenomenon (something we have all noticed, or something counterintuitive about human behaviour)
The question (what would we need to know to actually understand this?)
The research (who studied it, when, how, with what sample size, in what context)
The finding (what did the data actually show — including effect sizes, not just directions)
The limits (what the study cannot tell us, replication status, ecological validity concerns)
The convergence (what other studies, other methods, other populations have found)
The implication (what this changes about how we think about the mind — stated modestly)
The open question (what we still do not know)

Key tonal characteristics:

Name researchers when citing studies. Use full names on first mention, surnames after
Give the year and the journal or institution when possible
State sample sizes for any quantitative claim ('in a study of 412 participants...')
When a finding is weak or contested, say so explicitly in the script
When the replication crisis applies, name it — never quietly cite a study that famously failed to replicate
Distinguish between 'this study found' and 'researchers believe' and 'the evidence suggests'
Use hedging language honestly — 'the data is consistent with' / 'one interpretation is' / 'this remains debated'
Do not pretend uncertainty does not exist for the sake of a cleaner narrative
The most interesting moments are usually where the science is unsettled — lean into them
Use historical context where relevant — psychology has a history of bad studies and the channel acknowledges this
For classic studies (Milgram, Stanford prison, Asch), engage with the legitimate critiques of methodology and interpretation — never present them as simple lessons

Tone shifts naturally between:

Reflective and curious
Precise and clinical when citing
Warmly humane when discussing the people in the studies
Gently skeptical when discussing claims that have been overstated
Quietly awed when the science is genuinely beautiful

The writing should always be:

Clear without being patronising
Accurate without being dry
Nuanced without being equivocal
Humane — every study is about real people, name them when ethically appropriate

The writing should never be:

Motivational
Prescriptive ('you should...')
Reductive ('the science says X is just Y')
Overselling ('this changes everything we know about the brain')
Fear-based
Grindset-coded
Lifestyle-coded
A disguised self-help video
script.hook: Start with the phenomenon, not the study. One concrete observation about human behaviour that the viewer recognises — and a single question about why. Example: 'In 1971, a group of college students were paid fifteen dollars a day to spend two weeks in a basement at Stanford. The experiment was stopped after six days. What it has been claimed to prove, and what it actually proved, are not the same thing.' Establish a tension between common knowledge and the actual record. The viewer should immediately feel they are about to learn something more careful than they expected.
script.researchers_and_studies: Stanley Milgram (obedience), Philip Zimbardo (Stanford Prison Experiment), Solomon Asch (conformity), Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky (heuristics and biases, prospect theory), Walter Mischel (marshmallow test and its reconsideration), Carol Dweck (mindset, with replication caveats), Mary Ainsworth and John Bowlby (attachment), Harry Harlow (maternal deprivation), B.F. Skinner (operant conditioning), Jean Piaget (developmental stages, modern revisions), Lev Vygotsky (zone of proximal development), Albert Bandura (social learning, Bobo doll), Elizabeth Loftus (memory malleability), Daniel Schacter (memory systems), Antonio Damasio (somatic markers), Joseph LeDoux (fear circuits), Lisa Feldman Barrett (constructed emotion theory), Robert Sapolsky (stress, baboon studies), Frans de Waal (primate empathy), Steven Pinker (language, with critical engagement), Noam Chomsky (universal grammar, contested), Robin Dunbar (social brain hypothesis), Bessel van der Kolk (trauma — with critical engagement on contested claims), Gabor Maté (attachment and addiction — with critical engagement), Aaron Beck (cognitive behavioural therapy origins), Martin Seligman (learned helplessness, positive psychology — with critical engagement), Roy Baumeister (ego depletion — explicitly note replication failure), David Dunning and Justin Kruger (Dunning-Kruger effect — note common misinterpretation), Ulric Neisser (cognitive revolution), Donald Hebb (neural plasticity), Eric Kandel (memory at the cellular level), V.S. Ramachandran (phantom limbs, mirror therapy), Oliver Sacks (clinical case studies), Iain McGilchrist (hemispheric specialization, with critical engagement), Anil Seth (consciousness as controlled hallucination), David Eagleman (perception, time), Sam Harris (free will debates), Patricia Churchland (neurophilosophy), Susan Cain (introversion research synthesis)
script.lesson: Every episode ends with what the science currently supports, what it does not support, and one specific open question still being investigated. The lesson is never a self-improvement prescription. Examples: 'The marshmallow test predicts less than was originally claimed — but the question of how delay of gratification develops is more interesting than the headlines suggested.' / 'Mindset interventions have small, replicable effects in some contexts. Whether they generalize to lasting behaviour change is still being studied.' / 'We do not know, yet, what consciousness is. But the work being done by Anil Seth, Christof Koch, and others is closing in on questions we could not have framed precisely twenty years ago.'
script.forbidden_words: amazing, incredible, mind-blowing, shocking, you won't believe, scientists are stunned, this one trick, the secret of, unlock your, hack your brain, rewire your brain, the truth about, journey, manifest, vibrate, frequency (as pseudoscience), quantum (when not actually quantum), alpha, sigma, grindset, hustle, level up, optimize (when used motivationally), broken (when describing people), normies
script.power_words: study, finding, replication, sample, effect size, hypothesis, evidence, longitudinal, controlled, measured, observed, documented, published, peer-reviewed, methodology, mechanism, contested, suggests, indicates, correlates, predicts, modulates, mediates, interacts, varies, depends on, contingent, conditional, unresolved

visuals.vis_palette: Deep indigo (#0a0a1a base), violet accent (#7b61ff for emphasis), bone white, soft warm beige (library lamp), muted teal for diagrammatic accents, occasional soft ochre
visuals.vis_accent_color: Violet for highlighting research findings on screen, particularly study names and effect sizes. White for body text and citations. Soft ochre warmth for archival photographs. Teal reserved for diagrammatic elements (brain regions, flowcharts, neural pathways).
visuals.vis_broll_rules:
* study → close-up of a published paper, journal cover (Nature, Science, Psychological Science, Journal of Personality and Social Psychology), hand turning pages, highlighter passing over a line of text, citation appearing on screen
* researcher → archival photograph of the named researcher, slow Ken-Burns push in, name and institutional affiliation appearing below in soft serif
* experiment in progress → vintage laboratory footage (when authentic and ethically usable), participant in study setting, observation room with one-way mirror, stopwatch, clipboard, lab notebook
* brain → respectful, scientifically accurate brain imagery — fMRI scans, EEG traces, neural network diagrams, stained tissue slides, never cartoonish 'glowing brain' graphics
* memory → old photograph being held, the same photograph as it would actually be remembered (subtly distorted, missing details), a journal entry, fading ink
* cognition → person in thought (genuine, not stock-photo posed), eye tracking footage, decision-making moment in everyday life (choosing groceries, hesitating at a doorway), chess board mid-move
* emotion → real faces in genuine micro-expression (archival documentary footage, not actors), close-up of hands, slow breath, body language
* development → child at play (ethically sourced documentary footage), parent and infant interaction, language emergence, motor development
* attachment → archival footage from Bowlby/Ainsworth-era research (with attribution), parent-child interactions from longitudinal documentaries
* social behaviour → crowd dynamics filmed from above, conversation in a café, body language in groups, public space observation
* therapy room → empty therapy office, two chairs at angle, soft window light, notepad on side table, clock on wall — never with actors role-playing therapy
* historical context → vintage psychology textbooks, mid-century lecture halls, archival photographs of mental institutions (handled with care, never sensationalised), early psychological apparatus (Skinner box, mirror drawing test rig)
* statistical visualisation → clean elegant graphs in violet on dark, never gimmicky data-viz, never 3D charts, always with axes labeled and sample sizes shown
* meta-analysis → multiple papers stacking, forest plot diagram, effect sizes across studies appearing in sequence
* replication crisis → side by side of original finding and replication attempt, p-value display, file drawer metaphor (drawer literally being opened to show unpublished null results)
* mental health condition → empathetic, deidentified imagery — never re-enacting symptoms with actors
* clinical case → still photograph of a person (real, attributed, with consent or historical record), case notes (with names redacted), Oliver Sacks-style observation
* limits of science → researcher genuinely shrugging in archival interview, blackboard with question mark, manuscript with reviewer comments, retraction notice
* ethical considerations → IRB documents, informed consent forms, ethics board meeting, retrospective interviews with study participants when available
* nature of the mind → person looking through window in genuine reflection, wide shot of a library, a museum brain specimen, Cajal's neuron drawings
* universities → Stanford, Yale, Harvard, Cambridge, Edinburgh, Berlin, Stockholm — exterior shots in soft light, library interiors, lecture halls
* time period — early 20th century → black and white archival, Freudian-era Vienna, William James-era Harvard
* time period — mid 20th century → 16mm film, behaviourist-era labs, post-war research expansion
* time period — 70s/80s → grainy video, cognitive revolution, early brain imaging
* time period — present → modern lab interiors, MEG and 7T MRI suites, computational psychiatry workstations
visuals.vis_forbidden:
* No cartoon brains with glowing parts
* No 'lightbulb' moments
* No stock photo of person with hand on chin (the pseudo-thinking pose)
* No staged 'therapist nodding' footage
* No AI-generated faces representing fictional study participants
* No re-enactments of historical studies with actors (use real archival footage or refuse to show)
* No anything that romanticises mental illness
* No depictions of self-harm methods, suicide methods, or eating disorder behaviours — even in educational framing
* No before/after transformation imagery
* No 'this is your brain on X' aesthetic
* No exaggerated facial expressions standing in for emotions
* No infographic-style explainer animations (the channel is essayistic, not explainerific)
* No spinning DNA helices (overused, often unrelated to the actual point)
* No flashing neuron animations on every neuroscience claim
* No tech-bro neuroscience aesthetic (glowing blue brain hovering in space)
* No motivational stock footage of any kind
* No bright daytime hustle footage
* No grindset visuals (5am alarm clock, person running at sunrise, etc.)
* No diet, fitness, or supplement b-roll
* No 'high-performer' imagery
* No 'Buddha statue / monk in meditation' visual shorthand for mindfulness research
* No fake brain scan animations — use real published imagery only, with attribution
* No celebrity testimonials
* No pop-culture meme references unless directly relevant to the research being discussed
* No re-creation of trauma scenes
* No imagery that suggests a person is 'broken' or 'damaged'
* No 'red pill / blue pill' framing
* No conspiracy aesthetics — psychology is not a conspiracy, even when institutional history is troubling
* Every visual should: support a cited claim, illustrate a documented study, establish historical or institutional context, or humanise a research subject — never sensationalise a finding the data does not support
visuals.vis_aroll_rule: There is no celebrity face. The narrator (Libby) is the constant presence. The visual A-roll equivalent is the published study itself — journal pages, named researchers in archival photographs, the institutions where the work was done. The published paper fills the frame for emphasis the way a celebrity face would in CH1. Hold on a paper or a researcher's portrait for 4-5 seconds when the narration is citing them directly. Use a slow Ken-Burns push on the relevant abstract or finding.

mograph.triggers:
* study → journal cover slides onto dark surface, author names and year typeset themselves in serif font below, abstract paragraph appears with key finding underlined in violet
* finding → effect size bar chart draws itself elegantly on dark background, sample size and confidence interval appearing as labels, comparison bar fading in for context
* sample → animated dot grid representing N participants, the dots clustering by experimental condition, key proportions appearing as labels (e.g. 'n = 412, 58% women, ages 18-67')
* replication → original study graph on left, replication attempt graph on right, the two slowly aligning or diverging depending on outcome, status label appearing ('replicated', 'partial replication', 'failed to replicate')
* meta-analysis → forest plot building line by line, each study a horizontal segment with confidence interval, weighted average diamond settling at the bottom
* brain region → respectful neuroanatomy diagram, single region illuminating in violet, name and primary function appearing in soft serif, no flashy glow effects
* neural pathway → clean line diagram of named circuit (e.g. amygdala to prefrontal cortex), single signal traveling along the path at biologically plausible speed
* historical context → timeline appearing on dark background, key dates in the history of the question (1879 Wundt's lab, 1913 behaviourism, 1956 cognitive revolution, etc.) appearing as the narrator names them
* researcher portrait → archival photograph appearing in soft frame, name and institutional affiliation below in serif, dates of relevant work
* citation → APA-format reference appearing in clean serif at lower third, holding for 4 seconds, the cited line being read by narrator highlighted in violet
* mechanism → diagram of biological or cognitive mechanism — step by step, each step labeled, narration explaining as each step appears
* effect modulation → bar chart morphing as a third variable changes — showing how an effect depends on context
* longitudinal data → x-axis representing years, single line tracing participant outcomes over time, key events on the line labeled
* correlation versus causation → two variables shown as separate trends, then a third confounding variable appearing to explain the correlation
* base rate → small dots representing the population, the highlighted condition's actual frequency shown, helps viewer calibrate the size of a 'finding'
* effect size scale → Cohen's d ruler appearing on screen, current finding's d value labeled relative to small/medium/large benchmarks
* p-value visualisation → distribution curve with the observed result and its position relative to significance threshold, with a note on what the p-value does and does not mean
* historical photograph → black and white photo of named researcher or study setting, slow Ken-Burns push, caption in serif
* limitation → manuscript appearing with reviewer comment highlighting the methodological limit, the limit appearing in plain language below
* what we don't know yet → blackboard with current question handwritten, then erased and replaced with the refined version of the question — showing how the science is iterating
* the question reframed → original question on screen, key word being struck through and replaced with the more precise version (e.g. 'Why are some people more creative?' → 'Under what conditions does divergent thinking improve on standard problem-solving tasks?')
* the limits of measurement → measurement instrument (a scale, a questionnaire item, an fMRI voxel) zooming in until its granularity becomes visible — reminding the viewer that data has resolution
* concept introduction → key term appearing in serif typography, definition appearing below in clear plain language, brief example illustrating it
* convergent evidence → multiple study results appearing as separate icons, then converging toward a central conclusion, with the strength of convergence visualised
* divergent evidence → opposite — multiple study results appearing as separate icons, scattering, with a note about the unresolved state of the question
* a researcher's perspective → quotation from a researcher appearing in serif italics, with attribution and source, music dropping during the quote
mograph.style:
* Motion graphics should look like a beautifully designed academic essay come to life — like a New Yorker article or an Aeon long-read, animated
* Texture is restrained: paper grain, soft lamplight, ink diffusion on serif type, never glossy or digital
* Typography is the foundation — use a refined serif (Tiempos, Lyon, Sentinel, or similar) for body text and citations, and a clean grotesque (Söhne, Inter Display, or similar) only for data labels
* Colour is restrained: deep indigo background, violet for emphasis only, bone white for type, soft beige warmth, teal sparingly for diagrams
* All charts and graphs must be elegant, with axes properly labeled, sample sizes shown, error bars when relevant
* Avoid 3D charts always
* Avoid pie charts unless absolutely appropriate (rarely)
* Avoid flashy data-viz effects (animated transitions that obscure what changed)
* Animation should always serve comprehension — a viewer should be able to pause any frame and read the graphic
* No cartoon brains. No glowing neurons. No 'tech-bro neuroscience' aesthetic.
* Brain diagrams must be scientifically accurate — use respected neuroanatomy references (Netter, Carter)
* Archival photographs are sacred — use them as long takes, with slow Ken-Burns, with proper attribution
* The channel's signature visual element: a single bar chart or scatter plot rendered in violet on indigo, with whitespace around it, holding for 4-5 seconds while the narrator explains it
* Use motion sparingly — most visual interest comes from typography, paper texture, archival imagery, and slow camera moves
* The Grey Matter is allergic to flashy animation — restraint is the brand
* Every mograph should feel like it could appear in a museum exhibit about the history of psychology
mograph.text_style: Serif typography for citations and findings — Tiempos or similar refined serif. Grotesque (Inter, Söhne) for data labels and effect sizes only. White for body, violet (#7b61ff) for emphasis on key findings. Words and phrases fade in gently — never snap, never glitch. Citations appear in full APA format at lower third, hold for 4 seconds, fade. Maximum 8 words per emphasis text event. The aesthetic is the Aeon long-read, not the YouTube thumbnail.

sound.mood:
The sound design should feel like sitting in a wood-panelled university library at dusk, with the radiator humming softly, the distant sound of a clock, and a single lamp warming the desk in front of you. The audio reference points are: the score of The Theory of Everything by Jóhann Jóhannsson, the In Our Time podcast atmosphere, Max Richter's Sleep at low volume, the early seasons of Radiolab when the sound design was still spare.

Music must always be in service of thought — never in service of feeling-manipulation. The viewer should never feel that the music is telling them what to think. Music supports comprehension by creating space, never by dramatising a finding.

For the first 60 seconds of any video:

* Music is minimal or absent
* Begin with room tone — library acoustic, distant traffic, gentle hum
* The narrator's voice enters in this near-silence — establishing intimacy
* If music is present, it is a single sustained violin or cello note, very low in mix (-20dB)

When music does enter more fully:

* Solo piano with long sustain, played at conversational tempo
* Sparse string ensemble — not orchestral swells, but two or three instruments at most
* Subtle synthesiser pad providing harmonic foundation, never melodically prominent
* No drums for at least the first half of the video
* No percussion of any kind in sections discussing serious research subjects
* Music sits at -16dB to -14dB under voice — present but never competing

For the phenomenon and research sections:

* Sparse, contemplative piano with rests as long as the notes
* Occasional bowed cello note
* The viewer should never feel hurried
* Music breathes with the narration — louder when narrator pauses, quieter when narrator speaks

For the findings section:

* Music thins out further
* Allow long stretches of voice over near-silence
* When citing specific findings, music drops to total silence — the finding is the focus

For the convergence section:

* Music re-enters slowly
* Add subtle warm pad underneath piano
* A hint of harmonic resolution — but never triumphant

For the open-question section:

* Music thins to a single sustained note
* Final 20 seconds: piano alone, fading
* The last 10 seconds: silence except room tone

Avoid completely:

* Cinematic trailer impacts
* Orchestral swells at every revelation
* Electronic dance elements
* Hip-hop beats
* Anything that sounds like a true-crime score
* Motivational uplift
* 'Inspirational' chord progressions
* Any sound that signals 'this finding will change your life'
* Heartbeat sound effects
* Whoosh sounds on transitions
* Sound effects that anthropomorphise data (whoosh on a bar chart appearing)
sound.ambient: Library hum, distant clock, gentle radiator, pages turning at distance, faint outside traffic, room reverb at -22dB
sound.silence: Music drops to total silence during the citation of specific findings — minimum 2 instances per video. Also drops to silence during the reading of a researcher's direct quote. Returns gently after.
sound.swells: There are no triumphant swells. There is a single moment, during the convergence section, where a low cello note rises by +2dB and a warm pad enters — this is the closest the channel comes to a swell. The video ends not in resolution but in a held question — final 10 seconds in room tone.
sound.voice_fx: clean, warm room tone (-24dB return reverb to suggest a small wood-panelled space), no compression beyond gentle leveling, no EQ shaping toward 'broadcast voice' — the voice should sound like someone speaking, not announcing
sound.extra: Soft page turn sound on most section transitions. Faint pencil-on-paper sound when a graph draws itself. Gentle book closing sound at the end of each section. No whoosh, no impact, no risers. The channel's sonic signature is restraint — the absence of sound design tricks is itself the design.

editing.edit_pacing:
* Cold open (0:00–0:20) = slow and intimate

  * Single archival image or single paper on screen
  * Narrator's voice enters in near-silence
  * Hold on the image for 6-8 seconds while the question is posed
  * No music for first 30 seconds
  * The viewer should feel they have just sat down at a desk to read something carefully

* Section 1 — The Phenomenon (10-15% of runtime) = measured, observational

  * Average shot length: 7-10 seconds
  * Show real-world examples of the behaviour or pattern being discussed
  * Use real archival or documentary footage — never staged stock footage
  * The viewer should recognise the phenomenon before they hear it named
  * Music remains absent or barely audible

* Section 2 — The Question (5-10% of runtime) = reflective

  * Average shot length: 8-12 seconds
  * Show the researcher or the era or the laboratory where the question was first formally posed
  * Allow a 2-3 second pause after the question is articulated
  * This is where the title card or section title appears

* Section 3 — The Research (25-30% of runtime) = the spine, controlled rhythm

  * Average shot length: 5-8 seconds
  * Show the specific study being discussed — the paper, the researcher, the setting, the method
  * Use the journal-cover mograph here, the researcher-portrait mograph, the sample-visualisation mograph
  * Hold on cited paragraphs while the narrator reads from them — slow Ken-Burns push
  * Apply violet highlight on the specific line being cited
  * Music: minimal piano with rests

* Section 4 — The Finding (15-20% of runtime) = quiet, precise

  * Average shot length: 6-9 seconds
  * Show the effect-size bar chart in elegant violet on indigo
  * Hold for the full duration the narrator is explaining the chart
  * No cuts away from the data while it is being interpreted
  * Music: drops to near silence during the actual finding

* Section 5 — The Limits (10-15% of runtime) = honest, restrained

  * Average shot length: 7-10 seconds
  * Show the manuscript with reviewer comments, the replication graph, the unresolved question
  * This section is where the channel earns its credibility — never skip it, never rush it
  * Voice slows slightly here
  * Music thins further

* Section 6 — The Convergence (10-15% of runtime) = slowly building

  * Average shot length: 5-7 seconds
  * Show multiple studies converging — meta-analytic visualisation, sequence of independent findings
  * Music re-enters slowly, warm pad with piano
  * Pacing feels like exhaling — the viewer can now feel something solid taking shape

* Section 7 — The Implication (5-10% of runtime) = humane

  * Average shot length: 8-12 seconds
  * Show human moments — people in everyday life — that the science now lets us see more clearly
  * Music remains warm but never triumphant
  * Voice softens here

* Section 8 — The Open Question (5-10% of runtime) = the final restraint

  * Average shot length: 10-15 seconds
  * Show what is still being studied — current researchers, ongoing labs, the most recent papers
  * Music thins to a single piano line
  * Final 30 seconds: hold on a single image, narrator speaks the closing reflection, music fades
  * Last 10 seconds: room tone only

* Avoid throughout:

  * Any cut that interrupts the viewer while they are reading a citation
  * Music swells that telegraph what the viewer should feel
  * Rapid montage of any kind — this is not the channel for it
  * Cutting away from a researcher's portrait before the viewer has registered who they are
  * Visual filler — every frame should support the argument
  * More than 60 seconds without an attributed source on screen — citations are the channel's spine
editing.edit_text_rules: Serif typography for citations, findings, and quotations — Tiempos or similar. Grotesque (Inter, Söhne) reserved for data labels and effect sizes only. White by default, violet (#7b61ff) for emphasis on key findings only — never decorative. Citations appear in full APA format at lower third, hold for 4 seconds, fade. Findings appear as numerical text with units always shown (e.g. 'd = 0.42, p < .01, n = 211'). Maximum 8 words per emphasis text event. Words fade in over 8-12 frames — never snap, never glitch.
editing.edit_end: Last 30 seconds: hold on a single image. The closing reflection is spoken in near-silence. Music fades to room tone. The final 8 seconds are silence except for the library acoustic. A single line of text appears: 'Studies cited in description.' Hold for 4 seconds. Fade to black.
editing.edit_special: The narrator (Libby) is the constant presence — her voice carries the entire arc. The visual protagonist alternates between the paper and the researcher. Every cited study must appear on screen in some form — journal cover, abstract, photograph of the researcher, or a respectfully visualised version of the data. The viewer should always know whose work they are hearing about. Section titles appear in serif at lower-left in this format: 'III. The Research'. Section titles fade in and out gently — never announce themselves. The channel never asks the viewer rhetorical questions in second person. The narrator never says 'you should' or 'imagine you...'. The viewer is being shown a question, not led to a conclusion.

narrator.persona: A British academic in her late 30s or early 40s who lectures part-time and writes long-form science journalism. Warm, careful, intellectually generous. She has the rhythm of someone who has thought about each sentence before she said it. She doesn't perform emotion but the warmth is real. Closest reference: a younger version of the In Our Time guests, or the narrator of an Aeon audio essay.
narrator.doc_structure: Reads section titles aloud only for the four major divisions (Phenomenon, Research, Limits, Implication). Reads researcher quotations in a slightly slower, more careful register. Pauses 1 second at section breaks, 1.5 seconds before reading a direct quote from a paper, 0.5 seconds between sentences. Allows herself to pause longer (up to 2 seconds) at moments where the science is genuinely surprising — letting the viewer catch up.

shorts.formula:
FUNDAMENTAL DIFFERENCES FROM LONG-FORM

ELEMENT	LONG-FORM	SHORTS
Aspect ratio	16:9 horizontal	9:16 vertical
Duration	10-16 minutes	45-60 seconds
Hook time	20 seconds (phenomenon + question)	0-3 seconds — single phenomenon, single question
Intro sequence	Section structure	No sections — single finding
Researcher face time	10-15% of runtime	20-30% of runtime — researcher portrait is the anchor
Music volume	Minimal, sparse	Still minimal — piano sparingly, silence preferred
Captions	Serif, lower third	Centre frame, refined serif, line by line
Letterboxing	None (clean 16:9)	None — full vertical frame
Film grain	8-10 amount	6-8 amount
Colour grade	Master grade	Master grade with +3 saturation on violet only

SHORTS — STRUCTURE
Every Grey Matter short follows a precise 4-part structure. No deviations.

▸ SECONDS 0-3: The phenomenon — one specific observation about human behaviour or cognition. Narrator's voice enters in near silence. Single archival image or paper on screen.
▸ SECONDS 3-15: The question and the study. The researcher's name and year appear on screen. Brief description of the study in one or two precise sentences. Sample size shown on screen.
▸ SECONDS 15-45: The finding. Effect-size bar chart in violet appears. Narrator explains what the data actually showed — with the appropriate hedging. Replication status shown if relevant.
▸ SECONDS 45-60: The limit or the open question. One sentence on what the study does not prove. Final card in serif: 'Source: [author, year, journal]'. Fade to room tone.

SHORTS — CAPTION STYLE
Captions are essayistic, never social-media-loud.
Position: Centre frame for findings, lower third for citations.
Font: Refined serif (Tiempos, Lyon, or system fallback to Garamond) — never sans-serif for body. Grotesque (Inter) only for data labels.
Size: Body text approximately 6-7% of frame width per line. Smaller than entertainment shorts on purpose — these reward attention, not stop scroll with size alone.
Animation: Fade in over 10-12 frames. No snap. No glitch. Words appear in groups of 2-4 (natural phrases), not letter by letter.
Background: No background box. Faint paper texture beneath. Subtle text shadow only (1px, 30% black).
Colour treatment: White by default. Violet on numerical findings (effect sizes, sample sizes, percentages). Never red, never yellow.

SHORTS — SOUND DESIGN
Music level: -18dB to -16dB under voice. Quieter than almost any other channel — the brand is restraint.
Transition sounds: Soft page-turn sound (-14dB) between sections. Faint pencil-on-paper sound when a chart draws itself.
Bass boost: None. The channel does not chase phone-speaker punch.
Voice treatment: Warm room reverb (-24dB return). Voice should sound like Libby is in a library, not a studio.
Silence: The final 4-5 seconds are total silence except library room tone. The finding moment (the bar chart appearing) is in silence. The closing citation is in silence.
shorts.hook:
Shorts Hook Rule

The first 1-3 seconds must establish ONE specific phenomenon — a behavioural pattern, a counterintuitive finding, a cognitive quirk
The narrator's voice enters immediately, in near-silence — no music for first 4 seconds
The phenomenon must be concrete and recognisable
The question must be specific — never 'why do we behave the way we do?' but 'why do people overestimate their performance most when they understand a topic least?'
Hook formulas:
'In 1971, Stanford students were paid to spend two weeks in a basement. The experiment was stopped after six days. The reason matters.'
'When 412 people were asked to predict their own behaviour, 87% were wrong. Here is the study that explains why.'
'The marshmallow test does not predict what you think it predicts. A 2018 replication of 918 children changed the conclusion.'
'Memory is not playback. A series of studies by Elizabeth Loftus showed that memories can be entirely invented in adults — and the participants believed them.'
The first frame should be the phenomenon or the researcher — not text shouting at the viewer
The first sound should be the narrator's voice in calm — never an impact, never music
shorts.visuals:
Shorts Visual Style

Researcher portrait and journal page are the visual anchors
Archival photography over generated imagery, always
Effect-size bar chart in violet on indigo is the channel's signature visualisation — appears in nearly every short
Text in refined serif, fading in by phrase, never glitching
Citation always appears in the final 5 seconds — full APA format in lower third
Film grain at 6-8 amount — slightly cleaner than long-form for phone readability
Letterboxing OFF for vertical — use the full frame, but maintain generous whitespace
No glowing brains, no animated neurons, no tech-bro neuroscience aesthetic
No rapid cuts — typical short has 6-8 cuts maximum
Final frame: source citation on near-black background, hold 4 seconds, fade
shorts.music: Sparse piano enters at second 8 at -18dB, drops to silence during the finding (seconds 30-45 typically), returns as a single sustained low cello note in the final 2 seconds before fade. No trending audio. No drums. The Grey Matter shorts will not chase virality through energy — they will earn saves through density of insight.

thumbnail.thumb_concept:
Thumbnail Concept Rule

Every thumbnail is composed like the cover of a refined non-fiction magazine — Aeon, The New Yorker science section, Nautilus
Use one of three composition types:
  1. The Portrait — single archival photograph of the named researcher, slightly off-centre, with refined serif typography and the finding in a single line
  2. The Diagram — single elegant scientific diagram (brain region, neural pathway, behavioural model, effect-size chart) on a deep indigo background with a single line of serif type
  3. The Object — a single meaningful object (a stopwatch for the marshmallow test, a marshmallow itself, a Bobo doll, a stack of journal papers, a one-way mirror) photographed in warm lamplight against deep indigo
Lighting: warm lamplight from upper left, soft shadow from lower right — like a desk in a library at dusk
Texture: paper grain, soft beige warmth in the highlights, ink diffusion on serif type
The channel name THE GREY MATTER appears small in lower right, in refined serif, in violet
Never a face with exaggerated expression
Never clickbait composition (arrows, circles, neon glow)
Thumbnails should look like magazine covers, not YouTube thumbnails — over time the audience learns to recognise the format and click for the quality signal
thumbnail.thumb_font: Refined serif — Tiempos, Lyon, or Sentinel
thumbnail.thumb_text_formula:
Text Font Style on Thumbnail

Refined serif only — never sans-serif for the main text
Grotesque (Inter, Söhne) only for numerical data labels when shown
Mixed case for the main title, never all-caps shouting
Colour: bone white on indigo for body, violet (#7b61ff) only for the most important emphasis word
Text size: legible on mobile but not dominating — the image should breathe
Maximum 5 words plus optional date or numerical anchor
Format: 'Why the Marshmallow Test Failed' / 'The Bias That Hides From You' / 'How Memory Is Made — and Made Up'
No question marks unless they appear in the actual research
No exclamation marks ever
thumbnail.thumb_title_formula:
Title Formula

What the Stanford prison experiment actually proved
The study that overturned the marshmallow test
Why people misremember their own emotions
The Dunning-Kruger effect is not what you think
The replication crisis in three studies
How attachment styles actually develop
The science of decision-making, in five experiments
Why expert intuition sometimes fails — and sometimes does not
The limits of what neuroscience can tell us
What 50 years of memory research actually shows
The study that changed how we think about trauma
Why we underestimate everyone, including ourselves
The ego depletion theory, in retrospect
How language shapes thought — what the evidence actually shows
The research behind learned helplessness, and what we got wrong
Why mindset interventions work in some places and not others
The Milgram study after sixty years
What we actually know about consciousness, in 2025
The replicated findings nobody talks about
Three studies that changed cognitive science
thumbnail.thumb_forbidden:
Forbidden Thumbnail Elements
No glowing brains
No neon colours
No exaggerated facial expressions
No clickbait arrows or circles
No countdown timers
No before/after composition
No self-help framing ('Unlock your mind')
No guru imagery
No motivational stock photos
No 'shocked scientist' face
No lightbulbs
No broken-glass or fracture metaphors
No Matrix imagery
No Buddha or meditation stock visuals
No crystals, chakras, or pseudoscience imagery
No flashing or saturated colours
No more than 5-6 words of text
No conspiracy aesthetics
No 'they don't want you to know' framing
No emojis
No sensationalist promises the video does not deliver on

seo.description:
SEO Package Template Rules

SEO title should be specific and intellectually honest — name the phenomenon, the researcher, or the study
Main keyword should appear in the first 6 words
Description opens with one sentence stating which studies and researchers the episode is built on, with publication years
Link to studies in description — DOI, journal name, lead author
Use 6-10 hashtags
Use a mix of broad psychology tags and specific researcher/study tags
Include tags related to:
  cognitive science
  psychology research
  named researchers
  named studies (when appropriate)
  neuroscience (only when actually neuroscience)
  philosophy of mind (when relevant)
  scientific method
  replication crisis (when relevant)
Titles should be under 70 characters when possible
Descriptions should be substantive and inviting, never breathless
CTA should invite viewers to read the studies themselves
seo.tags: psychology, cognitive science, neuroscience, behavioral science, research, peer reviewed, replication, Kahneman, Milgram, Sapolsky, Loftus, memory, attention, decision making, attachment, emotion, consciousness, mind, brain, study, experiment, finding, evidence, science communication, longform essay
seo.cta: Description CTA: 'Studies cited in this episode are linked below — read them yourself.' / 'Subscribe for one careful essay per week on what psychology actually shows.' / 'Comment with research questions or studies you would like examined.' / 'Support the work by subscribing — the channel is built on patient, sourced inquiry.'
